narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71 average, a Year 3 decile of 3 compared to the usual 5.53, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 against an average of 3.21, an LSAT score of 33 below the standard 36.70, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 65% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test—high enough to trigger rejection of your application. This decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 academic decile, as your lower ranking there strongly signaled a higher risk of bar struggles compared to peers who ranked higher. Your family income bracket also played a notable role, pushing the prediction toward failure relative to typical distributions. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, with its below-average mark adding to concerns about long-term performance. Your Year 1 decile and LSAT score contributed further in the same direction, though less intensely, while your choice of full-time study offered a small counterbalance by aligning with the successful majority. Overall, these academic metrics painted a picture riskier than the profiles of students who typically pass the bar on their first try. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your upper-level grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience as a Black woman overcoming obstacles. Many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements—your path ahead is still bright if you address these key areas. We're rooting for you.
